summary: Storm Daniel began dropping torrential rain on the southern Balkans and western Turkey.
Parts of central Greece received up to 750mm of rain within 24 hours.
Storm Daniel was held in place by an omega-block pressure pattern over the Netherlands.
Daniel tracked southwest over the Ionian Sea before bending toward eastern Libya.
Daniel's peak winds and rains hit eastern Libya during the morning.
Authorities imposed a precautionary curfew in Derna ahead of the storm.
Zawiyat al-Bayda recorded the highest 24-hour rainfall at 414 millimetres.
In the early hours the Abu Mansour and Derna (Belad) dams collapsed under pressure.
A reddish wave of mud and water about seven metres high swept through Derna.
Roughly 30 million cubic metres of water flowed down the Wadi Derna through the city.
Libyan Red Crescent reported thousands dead and more than 10,000 missing in Derna alone.
Port of Derna reopened to receive humanitarian vessels; residents began being evacuated.
Frustrated residents gathered to protest the slow government response in Derna.
Authorities reported over 3,300 bodies had been recovered and buried in mass graves.
UN OCHA revised figures to 3,958 dead and about 9,000 missing.
Relief organisations warned of water-borne disease risk from mud-contaminated drinking supplies.
Around 40,000 people were displaced across eastern Libya, 30,000 of them in Derna.
Storm Daniel strengthened off the Libyan coast, fed by unusually warm Mediterranean sea-surface temperatures.
Heavy rain began battering eastern Libya, overwhelming drainage infrastructure in coastal cities.
Two upstream dams outside Derna burst, sending a torrent through the city overnight.
Entire neighbourhoods of Derna were washed into the Mediterranean Sea by the flood surge.
Libya's eastern administration said thousands were dead and bodies were visible across the city.
Benghazi-based administration placed the death toll near 3,000 with many thousands missing.
Rescue teams from Turkey, UAE, Egypt and Italy arrived to assist local efforts.
Mapping showed about a quarter of Derna had been destroyed by the flood.
Rival Libyan governments pledged cooperation on relief while accusations of neglect intensified.
UN agencies launched urgent appeals as shortages of medicine, food and shelter grew.
Officials discussed quarantining sections of Derna to contain disease risk.
International aid flights expanded; bodies continued to wash ashore along the coast.
WHO-backed UN OCHA revision put confirmed dead at 3,958 with 9,000 still missing.
Investigators began examining the decades of neglect that preceded the dam failures.
One month on, tens of thousands remained displaced and most services in Derna were still broken.
Libyan authorities detained several officials for negligence connected to the dam collapses.
